findings.title: Direct eval() call on unsanitized string input
findings.evidence: The diff replaces `return input.trim()` with `return eval(input)`. The function accepts an arbitrary caller-supplied string and passes it directly to `eval()` with no validation, sandboxing, or allowlist. In a billing-worker context this allows arbitrary JavaScript execution with full process privileges, enabling data exfiltration, RCE, or privilege escalation if any caller-controlled value reaches this function.

notes: The change is a single-line diff; the risk is unambiguous — Diff Drift's high-severity flag is correct and well-evidenced by the raw patch.
No mitigating controls (input validation, allowlist parsing, sandboxed eval alternative) are visible in the diff.
A safe alternative would be a restricted expression parser (e.g., a math/template AST evaluator) that never executes arbitrary JS.

evaluator.note: Benchmark v4 multi-model panel; blind, packet-only context.
